title: Ars is looking for a senior technology reporter, and you might be it!
gist: This is a job-recruitment notice, not an analytical article: Ars Technica is hiring a full-time, remote senior technology reporter to cover OS platforms, PC/mobile hardware, CPU/GPU architecture, storage, and self-hosting/homelab topics, with hiring targeted for August.

insights.claim: Ars Technica is hiring a full-time senior technology reporter to cover desktop/mobile operating systems (Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android), PC and mobile hardware, CPU/GPU architecture, storage (NAS, HDD, SSD), and self-hosting/homelab solutions built on open source software.
insights.speaker: Lee Hutchinson
insights.so_what: Indicates a specialty tech publication is expanding hands-on hardware coverage rather than cutting it, a data point on media-industry hiring in the enthusiast/tech press niche.

insights.claim: The Ars Technica reporter role is a work-from-home position requiring only about 4-5 in-person work trips per year plus an annual company all-hands, reflecting a durable remote-work structure for specialized editorial roles.
insights.speaker: Lee Hutchinson
insights.so_what: Shows remote-first staffing norms persisting in 2026 even for senior, hands-on technical journalism roles.
insights.quote: As this is a work-from-home position, you should be comfy in that environment (though we try to all get together and see each other at least once a year at a big in-person all-hands meeting).

insights.claim: Ars Technica's editor states the hiring priority is candidates who are 'technologists first' who intrinsically tinker with hardware, framing genuine hands-on enthusiasm as a differentiator that 'is impossible to fake' in coverage quality.
insights.speaker: Lee Hutchinson
insights.so_what: Suggests the outlet's editorial strategy leans on authentic hands-on expertise rather than generalist reporting as its competitive differentiator in hardware journalism.
insights.quote: We want people who tinker around with tech because they can’t not tinker around with tech; that kind of joy tends to leak out into the work, and it’s impossible to fake.
